title: LISS panel: personality ↔ political orientation
text: liss_long long form one row per (respondent × wave). CLPM need three waves two observed variables wide form. Bakker et al. use May 2008, 2013, 2019 personality waves. use three waves pair personality left–right item preceding December.
code: library(crossLagR) library(lavaan) library(dplyr) library(tidyr)  data(liss_long)

title: LISS panel: personality ↔ political orientation
text: closed-personality composite mean reverse-scored openness items plus conscientiousness items. compute simple per-wave mean ignoring factor model (book chapter fits measurement model instead).
code: open_items <- c("rich_vocab", "dffclt_undrstnd", "vivid_imagin", "no_interest",                 "excellent_ideas", "poor_imagin", "quick_undrstnd",                 "dffclt_words", "reflecting", "full_of_ideas")  cons_items <- c("always_prepared", "leave_belongings", "pay_attention",                 "make_mess", "chores_done", "forget_to_put", "like_order",                 "shirk_duties", "follow_sched", "exacting")  # Items reverse-scored in the LISS Big-Five battery (5-pt scale). open_rev <- c("dffclt_undrstnd", "no_interest", "poor_imagin",               "dffclt_words") cons_rev <- c("leave_belongings", "make_mess", "forget_to_put",               "shirk_duties")  reverse5 <- function(x) 6 - x  liss <- liss_long |>   mutate(across(all_of(open_rev), reverse5),          across(all_of(cons_rev), reverse5)) |>   rowwise() |>   mutate(     openness_high      = mean(c_across(all_of(open_items)), na.rm = TRUE),     conscientiousness  = mean(c_across(all_of(cons_items)), na.rm = TRUE),     closed_personality = mean(c(6 - openness_high, conscientiousness),                               na.rm = TRUE)   ) |>   ungroup()

title: LISS panel: personality ↔ political orientation
code: target_waves <- c(8, 13, 19)  wide <- liss |>   filter(wave %in% target_waves) |>   mutate(wave_label = match(wave, target_waves)) |>   select(case_id, wave_label, closed_personality, left_right) |>   pivot_wider(names_from  = wave_label,               values_from = c(closed_personality, left_right),               names_glue  = "{.value}{wave_label}") |>   transmute(     x1 = closed_personality1, x2 = closed_personality2, x3 = closed_personality3,     y1 = left_right1,         y2 = left_right2,         y3 = left_right3   ) |>   drop_na()  cat("Complete cases:", nrow(wide), "\n")

title: LISS panel: personality ↔ political orientation
code: clpm_fit <- lavaan(estimateCLPM(waves = 3), data = wide) summary(clpm_fit, standardized = TRUE)

title: LISS panel: personality ↔ political orientation
code: riclpm_fit <- lavaan(estimateRICLPM(waves = 3), data = wide) summary(riclpm_fit, standardized = TRUE)

text: Following @bakker_reconsidering_2021, CLPM cross-lags suggest modest reciprocal coupling closed personality right-leaning self-placement. RI-CLPM, absorbs stable -person trait differences random intercepts IxI_x IyI_y, typically reports much smaller within-person cross-lags — consistent idea two constructs correlated traits wave--wave drivers one another. per-trait breakdown (Big-Five facets × five political items), alternative specifications, discussion substantive implications, see book chapter.

text: Generates simulated longitudinal data following Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model structure. model includes stable -person traits (random intercepts) within-person dynamics (autoregressive cross-lagged effects).

title: Simulate Data from a Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) — baseRICLPM_sim
code: baseRICLPM_sim(   n = 500,   waves = 4,   ar_x = 0.5,   ar_y = 0.5,   cl_xy = 0.3,   cl_yx = 0.2,   cor_traits = 0.5,   innov_var = 0.5,   cor_xy = 0.3,   seed = NULL )

title: Simulate Data from a Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) — baseRICLPM_sim
text: n Integer. Number individuals/units simulate. Default 500. waves Integer. Number time points/waves. Default 4. ar_x Numeric. Autoregressive effect X (stability X time). Default 0.5. ar_y Numeric. Autoregressive effect Y (stability Y time). Default 0.5. cl_xy Numeric. Cross-lagged effect X Y (X_t-1 -> Y_t). Default 0.3. cl_yx Numeric. Cross-lagged effect Y X (Y_t-1 -> X_t). Default 0.2. cor_traits Numeric. Correlation stable traits (random intercepts). Range: [-1, 1]. Default 0.5. innov_var Numeric. Variance wave-specific innovations (within-person deviations). Default 0.5. cor_xy Numeric. Within-time correlation X Y innovations. Range: [-1, 1]. Default 0.3. seed Integer. Random seed reproducibility. NULL, seed set. Default NULL.

title: Simulate Data from a Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) — baseRICLPM_sim
text: tibble wide format columns: id Individual identifier (1 n) traitx Stable -person trait X traity Stable -person trait Y x1, x2, ..., xW Observed values X wave y1, y2, ..., yW Observed values Y wave

title: Simulate Data from a Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) — baseRICLPM_sim
text: RI-CLPM separates -person within-person variance: **-person level:** - Stable traits drawn bivariate normal distribution correlation   cor_traits **Within-person level (t >= 2):** $$X_t = \mu_x + ar_x(X_{t-1} - \mu_x) + cl_{yx}(Y_{t-1} - \mu_y) + e_{x,t}$$ $$Y_t = \mu_y + ar_y(Y_{t-1} - \mu_y) + cl_{xy}(X_{t-1} - \mu_x) + e_{y,t}$$ \(\mu_x\) \(\mu_y\) individual-specific traits (random intercepts), innovations \(Cor(e_{x,t}, e_{y,t}) = cor_{xy}\).

title: Simulate Data from a Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RI-CLPM) — baseRICLPM_sim
code: # Basic simulation with defaults data <- baseRICLPM_sim()  # Custom parameters data <- baseRICLPM_sim(   n = 1000,   waves = 5,   ar_x = 0.6,   ar_y = 0.4,   cl_xy = 0.2,   cl_yx = 0.15,   cor_traits = 0.4,   seed = 42 )  # Strong cross-lagged effects, weak stability data <- baseRICLPM_sim(   ar_x = 0.3,   ar_y = 0.3,   cl_xy = 0.5,   cl_yx = 0.5,   seed = 123 )

title: estimateALT, following Usami, Murayama, & Hamaker (2019) unified frameowk. — estimateALT
text: ALT model combines latent growth trajectories (intercept , slope S) autoregressive cross-lagged dynamics, following unified framework Usami, Murayama, & Hamaker (2019). Unlike LCM-SR (models dynamics residuals growth curves), ALT model applies AR/CL dynamics directly latent scores. growth factors ALT act accumulating factors – direct effects scores indirect effects lagged dynamics. Parameter labels follow unified naming convention: ar_x, ar_y: Autoregressive effects latent scores.     reflect carry-compounds growth trajectory. cl_xy, cl_yx: Cross-lagged effects latent scores. I_x, I_y: Latent intercept factors (accumulating). S_x, S_y: Latent slope factors (accumulating). d_var_x, d_var_y: Dynamic residual variances. d_cov_xy: Dynamic residual covariance. ALT conditions first observation (wave 1 exogenous). Growth factor loadings start wave 2.

title: estimateLGM — estimateLGM
text: function generates model syntax Linear Latent Growth Model (LGM) specified number waves. LGM models systematic change time using latent intercept slope factors. univariate models: - Intercept factor (): Represents initial level/starting point - Slope factor (S): Represents rate linear change time - Quadratic factor (Q): Represents acceleration/deceleration (specified) bivariate models: - components univariate model X Y variables - Covariances growth factors across variables - Optional constraints residual parameters Key parameters estimated: - mean_I_x, mean_I_y: Mean intercepts (initial levels) - mean_S_x, mean_S_y: Mean slopes (rates change) - mean_Q_x, mean_Q_y: Mean quadratic terms (included) - var_I_x, var_I_y: Intercept variances (individual differences initial level) - var_S_x, var_S_y: Slope variances (individual differences rate change) - var_Q_x, var_Q_y: Quadratic variances (included) - cov_IS_x, cov_IS_y: Intercept-slope covariances - var_p, var_q: Latent variable unique variances - cov_pq: Latent variable covariances (bivariate ) Time scoring options: - Default: 0, 1, 2, ..., waves-1 (intercept = initial level) - Custom: User-specified time points - Centered: Time scores centered around mean easier interpretation model assumes linear change time, optional quadratic component. Individual differences initial levels rates change estimated.

title: estimateLGM — estimateLGM
code: # Basic univariate LGM with 5 waves model_syntax <- estimateLGM(waves = 5) cat(model_syntax) #> # Latent Variables #> p1 =~ 1*x1 #> p2 =~ 1*x2 #> p3 =~ 1*x3 #> p4 =~ 1*x4 #> p5 =~ 1*x5 #>  #> # Latent Growth Factors #> I =~ 1*p1 + 1*p2 + 1*p3 + 1*p4 + 1*p5 #> S =~ 0*p1 + 1*p2 + 2*p3 + 3*p4 + 4*p5 #>  #> # Growth Factor Means #> I ~ mean_I*1 #> S ~ mean_S*1 #>  #> # Growth Factor Variances and Covariances #> I ~~ var_I*I #> S ~~ var_S*S #> I ~~ cov_IS*S #>  #> # Latent Variable Means (Fixed to 0) #> p1 ~ 0*1 #> p2 ~ 0*1 #> p3 ~ 0*1 #> p4 ~ 0*1 #> p5 ~ 0*1 #>  #> # Observed Variable Intercepts (Fixed to 0) #> x1 ~ 0*1 #> x2 ~ 0*1 #> x3 ~ 0*1 #> x4 ~ 0*1 #> x5 ~ 0*1 #>  #> # Latent Variable Variances #> p1 ~~ var_p*p1 #> p2 ~~ var_p*p2 #> p3 ~~ var_p*p3 #> p4 ~~ var_p*p4 #> p5 ~~ var_p*p5 #>  #> # Fix Observed Variable Residuals to Zero #> x1 ~~ 0*x1 #> x2 ~~ 0*x2 #> x3 ~~ 0*x3 #> x4 ~~ 0*x4 #> x5 ~~ 0*x5  # Bivariate LGM with custom time scores model_syntax <- estimateLGM(   waves = 4,   variable_type = "bivariate",   time_scores = c(0, 1, 3, 5) )  # LGM with quadratic growth model_syntax <- estimateLGM(   waves = 6,   estimate_quadratic = TRUE,   center_time = TRUE )

I_x, I_y, I_z: Stable trait factors (random     intercepts) representing time-invariant -person differences.     unique models include decomposition trait state (    direct analogue CLPM). Within-person latent deviations labeled p (X), q (Y), r (Z). structural latent variables direct analogue across models retain model-specific names.

title: estimateTSO — estimateTSO
text: simple variance-decomposition model bivariate panel case single indicator per wave per variable. observed variable decomposed time-invariant trait component time-varying state (occasion) component: $$x_{} = T_{xi} + s_{xit}$$ $$y_{} = T_{yi} + s_{yit}$$ trait factors T_x T_y capture -person variance, state residuals capture within-person variance. variance ratio var(T_x) / var(s_x) estimates --within variance ratio. model random-intercept-special case RI-CLPM (lagged dynamics), needed variance decomposition. single indicator per occasion, classic multi-indicator Cole-Martin-Steiger TSO identified, simplified version used. Parameter labels: T_var_x, T_var_y: Trait variances (-person). T_cov_xy: Trait covariance. s_var_x, s_var_y: State variances (within-person). s_cov_xy: Within-time state covariance.

title: monteCarloAllisonChamberlainFI — monteCarloAllisonChamberlainFI
text: trials Integer. Number Monte Carlo trials. Default 10. waves Integer. Number waves (time points). Must >= 3. Default 5. stability_p Numeric. Autoregressive effect x variable. Default 0.25. stability_q Numeric. Autoregressive effect y variable. Default 0.25. cross_p Numeric. Cross-lagged effect y x. Default 0. cross_q Numeric. Cross-lagged effect x y. Default 0. variance_p Numeric. Within-person variance x. Default 1. variance_q Numeric. Within-person variance y. Default 1. sample_size Integer. Sample size simulation. Default 2500. dgp Character. Default 0.5. variance_between_y Numeric. -person variance y (RI-CLPM DGP). Default 0.5. confounder_p Numeric. Effect confounder x (CLPMU DGP). Default 0.3. confounder_q Numeric. Default 0.4. include_confounder Logical. Include confounder CLPMU model. Default TRUE. confounder_type Character. "time_variant" "time_invariant". Default "time_variant". cov_pq Numeric. Covariance p q innovations. Default 0.1. verbose Logical. Print progress messages. Default FALSE. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: run_mc_sims — run_mc_sims
text: function performs following steps: Validates input parameters Creates validates parameter grid Adds default values missing parameters Loops parameter combination Calls appropriate Monte Carlo function Handles errors compiles results Returns combined data frame results Default parameter values: stability_p = 0.2 (autoregressive effect X) stability_q = 0.5 (autoregressive effect Y) cross_p = 0.0 (cross-lagged effect Y -> X) cross_q = 0.0 (cross-lagged effect X -> Y) variance_q = 0.5 (within-person variance Y) variance_p = 0.5 (within-person variance X) variance_between_x = 0.5 (-person variance X) variance_between_y = 0.5 (-person variance Y) cov_pq = 0 (within-time covariance X Y) data_generation = "clpmu", additional confounder parameters: confounder_p = 0.3 (effect confounder X) confounder_q = 0.3 (effect confounder Y) confounder_variance = 1 (variance confounder) confounder_stability = 0.4 (autoregressive effect confounder) include_confounder = TRUE (whether include confounder)

Compare regular RICLPM vs no-lag RICLPM results_regular <- run_mc_sims(   estimator = "RICLPM",   riclpm_type = "riclpm",   trials = 10,   waves = 4,   sample_size = 1000 )  results_nolag <- run_mc_sims(   estimator = "RICLPM",   riclpm_type = "riclpm_nolag",   trials = 10,   waves = 4,   sample_size = 1000 )  # Compare latent change models results_dual_change <- run_mc_sims(   estimator = "LCHANGE",   lchange_type = "dual_change",   trials = 10,   waves = 4,   sample_size = 1000 )  results_single_change <- run_mc_sims(   estimator = "LCHANGE",   lchange_type = "latent_change",   trials = 10,   waves = 4,   sample_size = 1000 )  # Study bias from unmeasured confounder results_confounded <- run_mc_sims(   estimator = "CLPM",   trials = 10,   waves = 4,   sample_size = 1000,   data_generation = "clpmu"  # Data has confounder, but CLPM ignores it )  # Compare estimators under confounding estimators <- c("OLS", "RICLPM", "CLPM", "RI") all_results <- lapply(estimators, function(est) {   run_mc_sims(estimator = est, trials = 5, waves = 3, data_generation = "clpmu") }) combined_results <- do.call(rbind, all_results) } # }

title: simLGM — simLGM
text: function simulates data Linear Latent Growth Model (LGM) : - Growth modeled using latent intercept, slope, optional quadratic factors - Individual differences exist growth parameters - Systematic change occurs time according specified growth functions univariate models: - Single variable measured across time points - Intercept factor determines initial level - Slope factor determines rate linear change - Optional quadratic factor acceleration/deceleration bivariate models: - Two variables measured across time points - Growth factors variables correlations - Residual covariances variables time point Parameter interpretation: - mean_I: Average initial level across individuals - mean_S: Average rate linear change - mean_Q: Average acceleration/deceleration (quadratic models) - var_I: Individual differences initial levels - var_S: Individual differences rates change - var_Q: Individual differences acceleration - cov_IS: Relationship initial level rate change - sigma2: Unique variance latent variables (measurement precision) - cov_res: Covariance latent variables (bivariate models) Time scoring options: - Default: 0, 1, 2, ..., waves-1 (intercept = initial observation) - Custom: User-specified time points unequal spacing - Centered: Time scores centered around mean

title: simLGM — simLGM
code: # Basic univariate LGM lgm_data <- simLGM(   waves = 5,   variable_type = "univariate",   mean_I_x = 10,   mean_S_x = 0.5,   var_I_x = 4,   var_S_x = 1,   cov_IS_x = -0.5,   sigma2_x = 2,   sample_size = 500 )  # Bivariate LGM with correlation between variables bivar_lgm <- simLGM(   waves = 4,   variable_type = "bivariate",   mean_I_x = 10, mean_I_y = 15,   mean_S_x = 0.3, mean_S_y = -0.2,   var_I_x = 2, var_I_y = 3,   var_S_x = 0.5, var_S_y = 0.8,   cov_I_xy = 1.2,   cov_S_xy = 0.3,   sample_size = 800 )  # Quadratic growth model quad_lgm <- simLGM(   waves = 6,   estimate_quadratic = TRUE,   mean_I_x = 20,   mean_S_x = 2,   mean_Q_x = -0.1,   var_I_x = 9,   var_S_x = 1,   var_Q_x = 0.01,   sample_size = 1200 )

title: simRICLPM — simRICLPM
text: Random Intercept Cross-Lagged Panel Model (RICLPM) decomposes observed variable : - stable -person component (random intercept) - time-varying within-person component (within-person factor) - Measurement error (fixed 0 perfect indicators) Variable naming convention: - X variables: x1, x2, x3, ..., x[waves] - Y variables: y1, y2, y3, ..., y[waves] - Z variables (included): z1, z2, z3, ..., z[waves] Model identification follows standard RICLPM practices: - Random intercept means estimated (unless estimate_means = FALSE) - Within-person factor means typically fixed 0 (representing deviations) - Random intercepts correlate first wave within-person factors - Observed variable residuals fixed 0 (perfect indicators)

title: simRICLPM — simRICLPM
code: # Basic RICLPM simulation riclpm_data <- simRICLPM(waves = 4, sample.nobs = 500) #> ✅ Successfully simulated RICLPM data with 4 waves and 500 observations.  # RICLPM with stronger cross-lagged effects riclpm_strong <- simRICLPM(   waves = 5,   omega_xy = 0.3,   omega_yx = 0.3,   sample.nobs = 1000 ) #> ✅ Successfully simulated RICLPM data with 5 waves and 1000 observations.  # RICLPM with third variable riclpm_3var <- simRICLPM(   waves = 4,   include_z = TRUE,   sample.nobs = 800 ) #> ✅ Successfully simulated RICLPM data with 4 waves (including Z variable) and 800 observations.  # RICLPM with unconstrained parameters riclpm_free <- simRICLPM(   waves = 4,   constrain_beta = FALSE,   constrain_omega = FALSE,   sample.nobs = 600 ) #> Warning: lavaan->lavSimulateData():   #>    some regression coefficients are unspecified and will be set to zero #> ✅ Successfully simulated RICLPM data with 4 waves and 600 observations.
